text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY CLINIC
POST-OPERATIVE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient — Post-Operative / Pathology Results Review

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Post-operative follow-up visit approximately three weeks after bilateral mastectomy. Review of final surgical pathology results and initiation of adjuvant therapy planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Marisol Fuentes-Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic postmenopausal woman with a diagnosis of stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, left breast, who presents today for her post-surgical oncology follow-up. She carries a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), which was identified at the time of diagnosis and factored heavily into her surgical decision-making.

By way of background, Ms. Fuentes-Delgado was initially diagnosed with a 3.4 cm grade 3 triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-) invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast with a single clinically suspicious ipsilateral axillary lymph node, confirmed by FNA. Her tumor was notable for a very high Ki-67 (92%), basal-like phenotype (CK5/6+, EGFR 2+), PD-L1 CPS of 28, and an HRD score of 62 on the FoundationOne CDx platform, consistent with her BRCA1 biallelic inactivation and SBS3-dominant mutational signature.

She received neoadjuvant therapy per the KEYNOTE-522 backbone: four cycles of dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide (ddAC) followed by twelve weeks of weekly paclitaxel, all concurrent with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every three weeks. Carboplatin was intentionally omitted from the taxane phase due to borderline renal function (CrCl approximately 52 mL/min at the time of that decision), which has since trended slightly lower. Her neoadjuvant course was complicated by grade 3 neutropenia after cycle 1 of ddAC (managed with filgrastim dose adjustment), grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism attributed to pembrolizumab (levothyroxine initiated), and grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy during the paclitaxel phase (dose reduced from 80 to 65 mg/m2; duloxetine started). Notably, midpoint breast MRI after four cycles of ddAC/pembrolizumab showed the primary mass down to approximately 1.6 x 1.4 cm from baseline 3.4 cm — a robust but incomplete radiographic response at that juncture.

Approximately three weeks ago, she underwent left modified radical mastectomy with immediate left tissue expander placement, contralateral prophylactic right mastectomy with immediate right tissue expander placement (elected in the context of her germline BRCA1 mutation after extensive counseling), and sentinel lymph node biopsy of the left axilla (3 nodes identified intraoperatively). She tolerated the procedure well; estimated blood loss was 280 mL with no intraoperative complications. Bilateral Jackson-Pratt drains were placed.

Today she returns for review of the final surgical pathology findings and to discuss the next phase of her care.

Current medications include: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, lisinopril (dose increased during neoadjuvant therapy for blood pressure management), metformin (dose adjusted during treatment; held on chemotherapy days per protocol), duloxetine 30 mg daily, ondansetron PRN nausea, and ibuprofen 400 mg TID (initiated for bilateral knee/wrist immune-related arthralgia during paclitaxel/pembrolizumab phase — patient reports this has largely resolved).

She denies fever, chills, wound drainage, or erythema at the surgical sites. She reports mild incisional tenderness bilaterally, improved from the immediate post-operative period. She continues to have mild tingling in her fingertips (residual neuropathy) and mild fatigue, both of which she says are tolerable and improving. She notes mild shortness of breath with exertion, which she attributes to deconditioning and surgical recovery, but this is new and warrants attention.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1-2, persisting but somewhat improved from peak treatment period. No fevers, no chills. Weight stable.
Cardiovascular: Mild dyspnea on exertion (new since surgery; see assessment). No chest pain, no palpitations, no orthopnea, no leg swelling.
Respiratory: No cough, no hemoptysis.
Breast/Surgical sites: Mild bilateral incisional tenderness, expected. No wound drainage, no skin breakdown, no erythema.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite returned to near-baseline. Bowel function normal. No nausea, no vomiting, no diarrhea.
Musculoskeletal: Peripheral neuropathy grade 1 — residual mild tingling in fingertips, stable. Bilateral knee and wrist arthralgia now grade 1 or resolved compared to prior visit.
Endocrine: Stable on levothyroxine; no symptoms of hypothyroidism (no cold intolerance, no constipation, no excessive fatigue attributable to thyroid).
Neurological: No new neurological symptoms beyond known residual neuropathy.
Psychiatric: Patient reports moderate anxiety related to pathology results and uncertainty about future treatment. Sleeping poorly. Acknowledges feeling overwhelmed.
Skin: Mild bilateral chest wall incision healing. Hair regrowth beginning (total alopecia from ddAC largely resolving).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: BP 138/84 mmHg (improved from prior visits, on lisinopril), HR 78 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Temperature 36.9°C, Weight 91.2 kg, BMI 33.1.

General: Well-appearing Hispanic woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears mildly fatigued but engaged and conversational.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Mild diffuse hair regrowth noted bilaterally. PERRL. Mucous membranes moist. No oral ulcerations.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable. Thyroid not enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No S3 heard. JVP not elevated.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. Good air entry.

Breast/Chest Wall: Bilateral mastectomy incisions are well-approximated and healing without evidence of infection, hematoma, or seroma. Tissue expanders palpable bilaterally and positioned appropriately. No skin erythema or warmth. JP drains have been removed per report; drain sites closed. No palpable left axillary adenopathy. Right axilla not assessed given prophylactic mastectomy.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No signs of lymphedema in either upper extremity at this time. Fingertip tingling reproducible on monofilament testing — distal sensory neuropathy pattern, grade 1.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait steady. Balance intact.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (from today's draw and recent prior results)

CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, improving from nadir during chemotherapy), Plt 241 x10^9/L, ANC 3.6 x10^9/L (recovered).
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 101, CO2 24, BUN 19, Creatinine 1.24 mg/dL (eGFR/CrCl estimated 47-49 mL/min by CKD-EPI — slightly below baseline of 52 mL/min, likely related to sustained NSAID use and underlying diabetic nephropathy; NSAIDs should be tapered and discontinued). AST 28, ALT 32, Alk Phos 88, Total Bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Albumin 3.9 g/dL.
Thyroid Function: TSH 3.4 mU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine 75 mcg).
HbA1c: 7.8% (slightly above target of 7.5% per endocrinology; ongoing co-management).
Lipid panel: Not repeated today.

Echocardiogram (performed approximately 2 weeks post-operatively per pre-operative plan): LVEF 57-58%. This represents a mild decline from baseline of 62% noted at initiation of chemotherapy and is consistent with the 58% noted at the pre-surgical assessment. No wall motion abnormalities identified. No pericardial effusion. Diastolic function mildly impaired (Grade I diastolic dysfunction). This has been communicated to cardiology and will be followed with surveillance echo in 3-4 months.

---

IMAGING

No new dedicated surveillance imaging today. Prior breast MRI (mid-neoadjuvant) and staging CT (at diagnosis) were reviewed in prior clinical encounters. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for post-operative baseline/surveillance will be ordered today at 6-month post-surgical mark. Echocardiogram results reviewed above.

---

PATHOLOGY RESULTS REVIEW

Final surgical pathology from the left modified radical mastectomy and sentinel lymph node biopsy has been reviewed in detail with the patient today. Key findings:

- Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, measuring 1.8 x 1.4 cm in the primary tumor bed (largest dimension). Treatment effect is evident, with fibrosis, foamy macrophages, and hemosiderin deposition occupying approximately 60% of the tumor bed; however, viable tumor cells remain in approximately 40% of the bed — this is NOT a pathologic complete response (pCR).
- Residual high-grade DCIS present, spanning 3 cm — also treated but incompletely eradicated.
- Lymphovascular invasion focal but present.
- All surgical margins are clear; closest margin is 5 mm.
- Sentinel lymph nodes: 1 of 3 positive, with a micro-metastatic deposit of 2.1 mm — no extranodal extension.
- Right prophylactic mastectomy: benign fibrocystic changes only. No occult malignancy identified — a reassuring finding given her germline BRCA1 status.
- Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) index: 2.4, classifying this as RCB class II — moderate residual disease.
- Pathologic stage: ypT1cN1mi (AJCC 8th edition), consistent with Stage IIA following neoadjuvant therapy.
- Immunohistochemistry on residual tumor confirms maintained triple-negative phenotype (ER-, PR-, HER2 1+ by IHC). Ki-67 on residual cells is 55%, down from 92% pre-treatment — indicating meaningful but incomplete treatment response.

I spent considerable time reviewing these findings with Ms. Fuentes-Delgado and her support person in attendance. She understood that while there was significant tumor reduction, the presence of residual disease (RCB-II) places her in a higher-risk group for eventual recurrence than patients who achieve pCR. I reassured her that there are effective adjuvant strategies designed precisely for this scenario.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, left breast, germline BRCA1-mutated (c.5266dupC), status post neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/ddAC/paclitaxel and bilateral mastectomy (left MRM + right CPM with bilateral tissue expanders). Pathologic stage ypT1cN1mi, RCB class II. Residual disease confirmed; no pCR achieved. Post-operative course uncomplicated to date.

2. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant — relevant for adjuvant therapy planning (olaparib eligibility per OlympiA trial), as well as cascade testing counseling for first-degree relatives.

3. Mild cardiac dysfunction (LVEF 57-58%, down from 62%) — likely anthracycline-related subclinical cardiotoxicity; under cardiology surveillance.

4. Grade 1 residual peripheral sensory neuropathy — stable and slowly improving on duloxetine. No functional limitation at current level.

5. Immune-related hypothyroidism — well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg, TSH within normal range.

6. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — HbA1c 7.8%, slightly above endocrinology target; ongoing co-management with endocrinology.

7. Hypertension — reasonably controlled on lisinopril; mild creatinine elevation likely related to NSAID use and underlying diabetic nephropathy; NSAIDs to be discontinued.

8. Anxiety and emotional distress related to cancer diagnosis and treatment — addressed below under plan.

---

PLAN

1. ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY — CAPECITABINE + PEMBROLIZUMAB CONTINUATION:
Given RCB class II residual disease following neoadjuvant therapy in a patient with TNBC, the standard-of-care approach per the CREATE-X and KEYNOTE-522 trial data is adjuvant capecitabine combined with continuation of pembrolizumab. We will plan for:
   - Capecitabine: Standard CREATE-X dosing is 1250 mg/m2 BID x 14 days q21 days x 8 cycles. Given her current renal function (CrCl approximately 47-49 mL/min — now below 51 mL/min), I am recommending a starting dose of 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 every 21 days with close renal monitoring. Renal function should be rechecked before cycle 1 and every 2 cycles. Dose will be adjusted accordingly. She will be counseled extensively regarding hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, mucositis, and the need to hold therapy at any grade 2 or higher skin toxicity.
   - Pembrolizumab: She received 8 doses during the neoadjuvant phase. She is eligible for up to approximately 9 additional doses to complete 1 year of total pembro per the KEYNOTE-522 protocol structure. Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks will resume concurrent with capecitabine, pending confirmation that no active immune-related adverse events are present at treatment initiation. Today's TSH is normal, and no active irAEs are identified.
   - Target start date: approximately 3-4 weeks from today, allowing continued surgical recovery and ensuring renal function has been reassessed.

2. ADJUVANT OLAPARIB — DISCUSSED BUT DEFERRED:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado has a germline BRCA1 mutation and HRD-high tumor, making her potentially eligible for adjuvant olaparib per the OlympiA trial (300 mg BID x 1 year), which demonstrated significant improvements in invasive disease-free survival in high-risk HER2-negative germline BRCA1/2-mutated breast cancer patients with residual disease or high pathologic stage. I reviewed this data with her in detail.

However, the patient expressed significant reluctance to add olaparib at this time. Her concerns center on polypharmacy burden (she is already on capecitabine, pembrolizumab, levothyroxine, metformin, lisinopril, and duloxetine), ongoing peripheral neuropathy that has not fully resolved, and overall treatment fatigue. I have documented her understanding of the potential benefit and her informed decision to forego olaparib at this time. I emphasized that this door remains open — olaparib could potentially be considered at a future juncture, particularly in the metastatic setting if recurrence occurs. We will revisit this decision at her 3-month follow-up and at completion of capecitabine.

3. POST-MASTECTOMY RADIATION THERAPY (PMRT) — REFERRAL PLACED:
Given the finding of a positive sentinel lymph node (micro-metastasis, 1 of 3 nodes), the patient meets criteria for PMRT to the left chest wall and regional lymphatics (including left axillary levels I-III, supraclavicular fossa, and internal mammary nodes) per ASCO/ASTRO guidelines. I have placed a referral to radiation oncology today. The radiation oncology team will complete their own consultation and simulation planning. PMRT should begin after adequate wound healing, typically 4-6 weeks post-surgery. Pembrolizumab will be held during radiation per our institutional protocol to avoid compounding radiation-related toxicity with immune activation.

4. CARDIAC SURVEILLANCE:
Echocardiogram showed LVEF of 57-58%, mildly decreased from pre-treatment baseline. Cardiology was notified. A repeat echocardiogram is scheduled in 3-4 months. No clinical heart failure at this time. Patient is asymptomatic from a cardiac standpoint (the mild dyspnea on exertion likely reflects deconditioning and post-surgical status rather than cardiac compromise, but will be monitored). Anthracycline cumulative dose was within standard limits; no further anthracyclines are planned. NSAIDs to be discontinued to reduce fluid retention risk and to protect renal function.

5. RENAL FUNCTION:
CrCl has trended from 52 to approximately 47-49 mL/min. Given sustained NSAID use and baseline diabetic nephropathy, I am discontinuing ibuprofen effective today. Patient counseled to use acetaminophen for pain/arthralgia as needed. Renal function to be rechecked in 4 weeks. Lisinopril to continue — it is renoprotective in the setting of diabetic nephropathy. BMP to be repeated at next visit.

6. NSAID DISCONTINUATION — see above. For residual arthralgia from prior immune-related joint symptoms, acetaminophen 500-1000 mg q6-8h PRN is appropriate. Ibuprofen is no longer appropriate given declining renal function.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM / ENDOCRINE:
TSH 3.4 mU/L — stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. Continue current dose. Thyroid function to be rechecked in 8 weeks or at next visit.

8. DIABETES:
HbA1c 7.8% — slightly above target. Will defer formal medication adjustment to endocrinology co-management team. Metformin use to be continued. Patient referred back to endocrinology for optimization prior to resuming systemic therapy.

9. GERMLINE BRCA1 — CASCADE TESTING:
Patient has been previously counseled regarding hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome secondary to her BRCA1 pathogenic variant. Reminded her to discuss first-degree relative testing (children, siblings) with genetic counseling. Genetic counseling referral confirmed previously in record; repeat contact provided for family members.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
Patient endorses significant anxiety and emotional distress related to her diagnosis, treatment course, and uncertain prognosis. She was close to tears during pathology review discussion, understandably. I spent significant additional time with her and her support person. She was reminded that RCB-II disease treated with appropriate adjuvant therapy carries meaningful, real chance of cure, and that we would be monitoring closely. Social work referral has been placed. Psychiatry referral for pharmacologic support of anxiety will be placed today given the intensity of her symptoms. Patient also made aware of BRCA1 peer support groups and a TNBC Foundation patient resource network.

11. ONCOPLASTIC / RECONSTRUCTIVE SURGERY:
Tissue expanders in place bilaterally and healing well per today's exam. Plastic surgery follow-up ongoing. Exchange to permanent implants anticipated in approximately 6 months pending completion of radiation therapy (radiation may affect implant planning; to be coordinated with radiation oncology and plastic surgery).

12. SURVEILLANCE IMAGING:
Baseline post-surgical surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis ordered. Will be obtained approximately 6 months post-surgery. No additional imaging in the immediate post-operative window unless clinically indicated.

13. RETURN TO CLINIC:
Patient will follow up in 3-4 weeks at which point capecitabine and pembrolizumab will be initiated (pending wound healing clearance, renal function recheck, and radiation oncology consultation). Labs to be obtained 3-5 days prior to next visit: CBC with differential, BMP, LFTs, TSH, HbA1c.

Time spent with patient: 65 minutes. More than 50% of visit time spent on counseling, pathology review, and shared decision-making regarding adjuvant therapy options including PARP inhibitor discussion.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Medical Oncologist]
Hematology/Oncology
[Institution]
